Probabilistic PCA

Prerequisites

This chapter builds on Multivariate Normal and Probabilistic Graphical Models. The EM algorithm and Gibbs sampling used for inference are formalized in the Expectation Maximization and MCMC chapters (Part III); the versions presented here are self-contained introductions.

High-dimensional data often has far less intrinsic variation than the dimension DDD would suggest. A class of students’ grade transcripts, or a collection of images, may vary along just a few underlying “axes.” Latent variable models make this intuition precise by positing that the high-dimensional observations xn∈RD\mbx_n \in \reals^Dxn​∈RD are generated from a low-dimensional latent variable zn∈RM\mbz_n \in \reals^Mzn​∈RM with M≪DM \ll DM≪D.

In this chapter we study the simplest such models, where the relationship between zn\mbz_nzn​ and xn\mbx_nxn​ is linear and all distributions are Gaussian:

  • Principal Components Analysis (PCA): two classical optimization formulations and their connection to the eigenvectors of the sample covariance matrix

  • Probabilistic PCA (PPCA): PCA as the maximum likelihood solution to a Gaussian latent variable model, enabling Bayesian inference

  • Factor Analysis (FA): a generalization of PPCA with per-dimension noise variances

  • Other linear LVMs: Independent Components Analysis (ICA) and Probabilistic Canonical Correlation Analysis (PCCA)

Source
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Principal Components Analysis¶

Motivation¶

Suppose we observe grade transcripts {xn}n=1N\{\mbx_n\}_{n=1}^N{xn​}n=1N​ where xn∈RD\mbx_n \in \reals^Dxn​∈RD is a vector of DDD grades for student nnn. We might want to:

  • Reduce dimensionality: are there a few axes of variation?

  • Visualize: embed the points in 2–3 dimensions for plotting.

  • Compress: summarize each student’s record compactly.

PCA addresses all three. There are two classical equivalent formulations.

Maximum Variance Formulation¶

Goal: project the data onto an MMM-dimensional subspace while maximizing the variance of the projected data.

For M=1M = 1M=1, let u1∈RD\mbu_1 \in \reals^Du1​∈RD be a unit vector defining the subspace. The projection of xn\mbx_nxn​ is the scalar u1⊤xn\mbu_1^\top \mbx_nu1⊤​xn​ (the score or embedding). Its variance over the NNN data points is:

1N∑n=1N[u1⊤xn−u1⊤xˉ]2=u1⊤Su1,\begin{align} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N \bigl[\mbu_1^\top \mbx_n - \mbu_1^\top \bar{\mbx}\bigr]^2 = \mbu_1^\top \mbS \mbu_1, \end{align}N1​n=1∑N​[u1⊤​xn​−u1⊤​xˉ]2=u1⊤​Su1​,​​
(1)

where xˉ=1N∑nxn\bar{\mbx} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_n \mbx_nxˉ=N1​∑n​xn​ is the sample mean and S=1N∑n(xn−xˉ)(xn−xˉ)⊤\mbS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_n (\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})(\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})^\topS=N1​∑n​(xn​−xˉ)(xn​−xˉ)⊤ is the sample covariance matrix.

Maximizing u1⊤Su1\mbu_1^\top \mbS \mbu_1u1⊤​Su1​ subject to ∥u1∥2=1\|\mbu_1\|^2 = 1∥u1​∥2=1 via a Lagrange multiplier gives Su1=λ1u1\mbS \mbu_1 = \lambda_1 \mbu_1Su1​=λ1​u1​, so u1\mbu_1u1​ must be an eigenvector of S\mbSS. Left-multiplying by u1⊤\mbu_1^\topu1⊤​ shows the projected variance equals λ1\lambda_1λ1​, so we should choose the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue.

More generally, the MMM-dimensional principal subspace is spanned by the MMM eigenvectors u1,…,uM\mbu_1, \ldots, \mbu_Mu1​,…,uM​ with the largest eigenvalues λ1≥⋯≥λM\lambda_1 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_Mλ1​≥⋯≥λM​.

Linear Autoencoder Formulation¶

Goal: find an orthogonal matrix W∈RD×M\mbW \in \reals^{D \times M}W∈RD×M (with W⊤W=IM\mbW^\top \mbW = \mbI_MW⊤W=IM​) that minimizes mean squared reconstruction error.

We encode as zn=W⊤(xn−xˉ)\mbz_n = \mbW^\top (\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})zn​=W⊤(xn​−xˉ) and decode as x^n=Wzn+xˉ\hat{\mbx}_n = \mbW \mbz_n + \bar{\mbx}x^n​=Wzn​+xˉ. The loss is:

L(W)=1N∑n=1N∥xn−x^n∥2=Tr[(I−WW⊤)S(I−WW⊤)]=Tr[S]−Tr[W⊤SW].\begin{align} \mathcal{L}(\mbW) &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^N \|\mbx_n - \hat{\mbx}_n\|^2 = \Tr\bigl[(\mbI - \mbW\mbW^\top)\mbS(\mbI - \mbW\mbW^\top)\bigr] = \Tr[\mbS] - \Tr[\mbW^\top \mbS \mbW]. \end{align}L(W)​=N1​n=1∑N​∥xn​−x^n​∥2=Tr[(I−WW⊤)S(I−WW⊤)]=Tr[S]−Tr[W⊤SW].​​
(2)

Minimizing L(W)\mathcal{L}(\mbW)L(W) is equivalent to maximizing Tr[W⊤SW]=∑m=1Mλm(wm⊤um)2\Tr[\mbW^\top \mbS \mbW] = \sum_{m=1}^M \lambda_m (\mbw_m^\top \mbu_m)^2Tr[W⊤SW]=∑m=1M​λm​(wm⊤​um​)2, which is again solved by W=UM\mbW = \mbU_MW=UM​, the leading MMM eigenvectors.

PCA and the Singular Value Decomposition¶

Let Y=1NXc\mbY = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \mbX_cY=N​1​Xc​ where Xc\mbX_cXc​ has rows (xn−xˉ)⊤(\mbx_n - \bar{\mbx})^\top(xn​−xˉ)⊤. Then Y⊤Y=S\mbY^\top \mbY = \mbSY⊤Y=S.

The SVD of Y=VΛ1/2U⊤\mbY = \mbV \mbLambda^{1/2} \mbU^\topY=VΛ1/2U⊤ reveals:

  • Right singular vectors of Y\mbYY (columns of U\mbUU) = eigenvectors of S\mbSS = principal components.

  • Squared singular values = eigenvalues λ1,…,λD\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_Dλ1​,…,λD​ of S\mbSS.

In practice, PCA is computed via SVD of Y\mbYY rather than by eigendecomposing S\mbSS, since SVD is numerically more stable.

Explained Variance¶

The fraction of total variance captured by MMM principal components is:

variance explained=∑m=1Mλm∑d=1Dλd.\begin{align} \text{variance explained} = \frac{\sum_{m=1}^M \lambda_m}{\sum_{d=1}^D \lambda_d}. \end{align}variance explained=∑d=1D​λd​∑m=1M​λm​​.​​
(3)

A scree plot shows this quantity (per-component and cumulative) as a function of MMM. Below we generate synthetic data from a rank-3 linear Gaussian model and visualize the scree plot.

# Generate synthetic data from a rank-3 linear Gaussian model
torch.manual_seed(305)
N, D, M_true = 300, 20, 3   # datapoints, features, true latent dims
W_true = torch.randn(D, M_true)         # D x M weight matrix
Z_true = torch.randn(N, M_true)         # N x M latent variables
σ2     = torch.tensor(0.5)              # noise variance
X = Z_true @ W_true.T + torch.sqrt(σ2) * torch.randn(N, D)

# Centre the data and compute PCA via SVD
μ  = X.mean(0)                          # sample mean, shape (D,)
Xc = X - μ                              # centred data, shape (N, D)
Y  = Xc / N**0.5                        # Y.T @ Y = sample covariance S
_, s, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(Y, full_matrices=False)
UM  = Vt.T                              # principal components, columns of UM
λ   = s**2                              # eigenvalues of S (variances)
Source
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4))

pct = 100 * λ / λ.sum()
axs[0].bar(range(1, D+1), pct.numpy(), color='steelblue', alpha=0.8)
axs[0].set_xlabel('Principal component')
axs[0].set_ylabel('Variance explained (%)')
axs[0].set_title('Per-component variance')

axs[1].plot(range(1, D+1), torch.cumsum(pct, 0).numpy(), 'o-', color='steelblue')
axs[1].axhline(90, color='r', ls='--', lw=1, label='90%')
axs[1].set_xlabel('Number of components')
axs[1].set_ylabel('Cumulative variance explained (%)')
axs[1].set_title('Scree plot')
axs[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()

Probabilistic PCA¶

Model¶

The two classical formulations above cast PCA as an optimization problem. Probabilistic PCA (PPCA) instead defines a joint distribution over xn\mbx_nxn​ and a latent variable zn\mbz_nzn​:

zn∼iidN(0,IM)xn∣zn∼N(Wzn+μ,  σ2ID),\begin{align} \mbz_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_M) \\ \mbx_n \mid \mbz_n &\sim \cN(\mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu,\; \sigma^2 \mbI_D), \end{align}zn​xn​∣zn​​∼iidN(0,IM​)∼N(Wzn​+μ,σ2ID​),​​
(4)

where W∈RD×M\mbW \in \reals^{D \times M}W∈RD×M are the loadings (analogous to the principal components), μ∈RD\mbmu \in \reals^Dμ∈RD is the mean, and σ2\sigma^2σ2 is an isotropic noise variance.

Equivalently, xn=Wzn+μ+ϵn\mbx_n = \mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu + \mbepsilon_nxn​=Wzn​+μ+ϵn​ with ϵn∼N(0,σ2ID)\mbepsilon_n \sim \cN(\mbzero, \sigma^2 \mbI_D)ϵn​∼N(0,σ2ID​).

The marginal distribution of xn\mbx_nxn​ is:

p(xn∣W,μ,σ2)=N(xn∣μ,  WW⊤+σ2ID⏟C),\begin{align} p(\mbx_n \mid \mbW, \mbmu, \sigma^2) = \cN(\mbx_n \mid \mbmu,\; \underbrace{\mbW\mbW^\top + \sigma^2 \mbI_D}_{\mbC}), \end{align}p(xn​∣W,μ,σ2)=N(xn​∣μ,CWW⊤+σ2ID​​​),​​
(5)

a Gaussian with low-rank plus diagonal covariance — only O(MD)O(MD)O(MD) parameters instead of O(D2)O(D^2)O(D2).

Maximum Likelihood Estimation¶

The marginal log-likelihood is:

L(W,μ,σ2)=−ND2log⁡2π−N2log⁡∣C∣−12∑n=1N(xn−μ)⊤C−1(xn−μ).\begin{align} \mathcal{L}(\mbW, \mbmu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{ND}{2}\log 2\pi - \frac{N}{2}\log|\mbC| - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \mbC^{-1} (\mbx_n - \mbmu). \end{align}L(W,μ,σ2)=−2ND​log2π−2N​log∣C∣−21​n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)⊤C−1(xn​−μ).​​
(6)

Tipping and Bishop (1999) showed the MLE has a closed form:

μML=xˉ,WML=UM(ΛM−σ2I)1/2R,σML2=1D−M∑m=M+1Dλm,\begin{align} \mbmu_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \bar{\mbx}, \\ \mbW_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \mbU_M (\mbLambda_M - \sigma^2 \mbI)^{1/2} \mbR, \\ \sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \frac{1}{D - M} \sum_{m=M+1}^D \lambda_m, \end{align}μML​WML​σML2​​=xˉ,=UM​(ΛM​−σ2I)1/2R,=D−M1​m=M+1∑D​λm​,​​
(7)

where UM\mbU_MUM​ contains the MMM leading eigenvectors of the sample covariance S\mbSS, ΛM=diag(λ1,…,λM)\mbLambda_M = \diag(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_M)ΛM​=diag(λ1​,…,λM​), and R∈RM×M\mbR \in \reals^{M \times M}R∈RM×M is an arbitrary orthogonal matrix.

The weights are identifiable only up to orthogonal rotation — only the subspace spanned by UM\mbU_MUM​ is uniquely determined. The MLE noise variance σML2\sigma^2_{\mathsf{ML}}σML2​ is the average variance discarded in the trailing D−MD - MD−M dimensions.

Posterior Distribution on Latent Variables¶

Given parameters W\mbWW, μ\mbmuμ, σ2\sigma^2σ2, the posterior on zn\mbz_nzn​ combines the Gaussian prior with the Gaussian likelihood:

p(zn∣xn,W,μ,σ2)=N(zn∣J−1hn,  J−1),\begin{align} p(\mbz_n \mid \mbx_n, \mbW, \mbmu, \sigma^2) = \cN(\mbz_n \mid \mbJ^{-1}\mbh_n,\; \mbJ^{-1}), \end{align}p(zn​∣xn​,W,μ,σ2)=N(zn​∣J−1hn​,J−1),​​
(8)

where

J=IM+1σ2W⊤W∈RM×M,hn=1σ2W⊤(xn−μ)∈RM.\begin{align} \mbJ &= \mbI_M + \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \mbW^\top \mbW \in \reals^{M \times M}, \qquad \mbh_n = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \mbW^\top (\mbx_n - \mbmu) \in \reals^M. \end{align}J​=IM​+σ21​W⊤W∈RM×M,hn​=σ21​W⊤(xn​−μ)∈RM.​​
(9)

Note that the posterior precision matrix J\mbJJ is the same for all nnn; only the information vector hn\mbh_nhn​ varies. The M×MM \times MM×M system is much cheaper to invert than the D×DD \times DD×D marginal covariance.

Zero-noise limit: as σ2→0\sigma^2 \to 0σ2→0, the posterior mean converges to (W⊤W)−1W⊤(xn−μ)(\mbW^\top \mbW)^{-1} \mbW^\top (\mbx_n - \mbmu)(W⊤W)−1W⊤(xn​−μ), the ordinary least-squares projection — identical to classical PCA (up to scaling).

# MLE for PPCA with M components
M_fit  = 3
UM_fit = Vt[:M_fit].T              # D x M leading principal components
ΛM     = torch.diag(λ[:M_fit])    # M x M eigenvalue matrix
σ2_ML  = λ[M_fit:].mean()         # MLE noise variance

# MLE weights (orthogonal rotation R = I)
W_ML = UM_fit @ (ΛM - σ2_ML * torch.eye(M_fit)).sqrt()

# Posterior on latent variables
J     = torch.eye(M_fit) + (1/σ2_ML) * W_ML.T @ W_ML   # M x M precision
Σz    = torch.linalg.inv(J)                              # M x M posterior cov
H     = Xc @ W_ML / σ2_ML                               # N x M info vectors
Z_hat = H @ Σz.T                                         # N x M posterior means

print(f'MLE noise variance σ²  = {σ2_ML:.3f}  (true = {σ2.item():.3f})')
print(f'Posterior covariance Σz:\n{Σz.numpy().round(3)}')
MLE noise variance σ²  = 0.483  (true = 0.500)
Posterior covariance Σz:
[[ 0.024  0.    -0.   ]
 [ 0.     0.038  0.   ]
 [-0.     0.     0.082]]
Source
# Visualize the 2D posterior mean embedding
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))
ax.scatter(Z_hat[:, 0].numpy(), Z_hat[:, 1].numpy(),
           c=Z_true[:, 0].numpy(), cmap='coolwarm', s=20, alpha=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\hat{z}_1$ (posterior mean)')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\hat{z}_2$ (posterior mean)')
ax.set_title('PPCA posterior mean embeddings')
plt.tight_layout()

Gibbs Sampling for Probabilistic PCA¶

If we want a fully Bayesian treatment, we place a prior on the parameters. For simplicity assume μ=0\mbmu = \mbzeroμ=0 (center the data first) and put:

p(W,σ2)=χ−2(σ2∣ν0,σ02)∏d=1DN(wd∣0, σ2κ0IM),\begin{align} p(\mbW, \sigma^2) = \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_0, \sigma_0^2) \prod_{d=1}^D \cN(\mbw_d \mid \mbzero,\, \tfrac{\sigma^2}{\kappa_0} \mbI_M), \end{align}p(W,σ2)=χ−2(σ2∣ν0​,σ02​)d=1∏D​N(wd​∣0,κ0​σ2​IM​),​​
(10)

where wd∈RM\mbw_d \in \reals^Mwd​∈RM is the ddd-th row of W\mbWW.

Complete conditional for each row wd\mbw_dwd​: combining the row prior with the NNN likelihoods for the ddd-th output gives a Gaussian:

p(wd∣{zn,xn},σ2)=N(wd∣Jd−1hd,  Jd−1),\begin{align} p(\mbw_d \mid \{\mbz_n, \mbx_n\}, \sigma^2) = \cN(\mbw_d \mid \mbJ_d^{-1} \mbh_d,\; \mbJ_d^{-1}), \end{align}p(wd​∣{zn​,xn​},σ2)=N(wd​∣Jd−1​hd​,Jd−1​),​​
(11)

where

Jd=κ0σ2IM+1σ2∑n=1Nznzn⊤,hd=1σ2∑n=1Nxn,d zn.\begin{align} \mbJ_d &= \frac{\kappa_0}{\sigma^2} \mbI_M + \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{n=1}^N \mbz_n \mbz_n^\top, \qquad \mbh_d = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{n=1}^N x_{n,d}\, \mbz_n. \end{align}Jd​​=σ2κ0​​IM​+σ21​n=1∑N​zn​zn⊤​,hd​=σ21​n=1∑N​xn,d​zn​.​​
(12)

Complete conditional for σ2\sigma^2σ2: conjugate with the scaled inverse chi-squared family:

p(σ2∣{zn,xn},W)=χ−2(σ2∣νN,σN2),νN=ν0+DM+DN,σN2=1νN ⁣[ν0σ02+κ0∥W∥F2+∑n=1N∥xn−Wzn∥2].\begin{align} p(\sigma^2 \mid \{\mbz_n, \mbx_n\}, \mbW) &= \chi^{-2}(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_N, \sigma_N^2), \\ \nu_N &= \nu_0 + DM + DN, \\ \sigma_N^2 &= \frac{1}{\nu_N}\!\left[\nu_0 \sigma_0^2 + \kappa_0 \|\mbW\|_F^2 + \sum_{n=1}^N \|\mbx_n - \mbW \mbz_n\|^2\right]. \end{align}p(σ2∣{zn​,xn​},W)νN​σN2​​=χ−2(σ2∣νN​,σN2​),=ν0​+DM+DN,=νN​1​[ν0​σ02​+κ0​∥W∥F2​+n=1∑N​∥xn​−Wzn​∥2].​​
(13)

Complete conditional for zn\mbz_nzn​: same as the PPCA posterior derived above.

Connection to Bayesian linear regression

Each row update p(wd∣{zn,xn,d},σ2)p(\mbw_d \mid \{\mbz_n, x_{n,d}\}, \sigma^2)p(wd​∣{zn​,xn,d​},σ2) is exactly Bayesian linear regression — predict scalar xn,dx_{n,d}xn,d​ from features zn\mbz_nzn​ with a Gaussian prior on coefficients wd\mbw_dwd​. The Gibbs sampler alternates between updating the “regressors” (zn\mbz_nzn​, which play the role of features) and the “weights” (W\mbWW).

Factor Analysis¶

Factor analysis (FA) relaxes the isotropic noise assumption of PPCA by allowing each output dimension to have its own noise variance:

zn∼iidN(0,IM)xn∣zn∼N(Wzn+μ,  diag(σ2)),\begin{align} \mbz_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_M) \\ \mbx_n \mid \mbz_n &\sim \cN(\mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu,\; \diag(\mbsigma^2)), \end{align}zn​xn​∣zn​​∼iidN(0,IM​)∼N(Wzn​+μ,diag(σ2)),​​
(14)

where σ2=[σ12,…,σD2]⊤\mbsigma^2 = [\sigma_1^2, \ldots, \sigma_D^2]^\topσ2=[σ12​,…,σD2​]⊤. The marginal covariance is C=WW⊤+diag(σ2)\mbC = \mbW\mbW^\top + \diag(\mbsigma^2)C=WW⊤+diag(σ2) — still low-rank plus diagonal, but with DDD independent noise scales instead of one.

With the per-dimension prior:

p(W,σ2)=∏d=1D[χ−2(σd2∣ν0,σ02) N(wd∣0, σd2κ0IM)],\begin{align} p(\mbW, \mbsigma^2) = \prod_{d=1}^D \left[ \chi^{-2}(\sigma_d^2 \mid \nu_0, \sigma_0^2)\, \cN(\mbw_d \mid \mbzero,\, \tfrac{\sigma_d^2}{\kappa_0} \mbI_M) \right], \end{align}p(W,σ2)=d=1∏D​[χ−2(σd2​∣ν0​,σ02​)N(wd​∣0,κ0​σd2​​IM​)],​​
(15)

the Gibbs updates for each (wd,σd2)(\mbw_d, \sigma_d^2)(wd​,σd2​) pair are identical to those for PPCA but with σd2\sigma_d^2σd2​ in place of the shared σ2\sigma^2σ2. All rows are independent given {zn}\{\mbz_n\}{zn​}, so they can be updated in parallel.

Other Linear Latent Variable Models¶

Independent Components Analysis¶

ICA replaces the Gaussian latent prior with a product of non-Gaussian marginals:

zn∼iidp(z)=∏m=1Mp(zm),xn∣zn∼N(Wzn+μ,  diag(σ2)).\begin{align} \mbz_n &\iid{\sim} p(\mbz) = \prod_{m=1}^M p(z_m), \\ \mbx_n \mid \mbz_n &\sim \cN(\mbW \mbz_n + \mbmu,\; \diag(\mbsigma^2)). \end{align}zn​xn​∣zn​​∼iidp(z)=m=1∏M​p(zm​),∼N(Wzn​+μ,diag(σ2)).​​
(16)

The non-Gaussian prior is essential: if p(z)p(\mbz)p(z) were Gaussian, any correlation in p(z)p(\mbz)p(z) could be absorbed into W\mbWW, reducing the model to factor analysis. A common choice is a Laplace (heavy-tailed) prior, p(zm)∝e−∣zm∣p(z_m) \propto e^{-|z_m|}p(zm​)∝e−∣zm​∣, which encourages the latent factors to be sparse.

ICA is widely used in signal processing (e.g., separating audio sources) and neuroscience.

Probabilistic Canonical Correlation Analysis¶

Suppose we observe two data modalities, xn∈RDx\mbx_n \in \reals^{D_x}xn​∈RDx​ and yn∈RDy\mby_n \in \reals^{D_y}yn​∈RDy​. Probabilistic CCA posits:

zn(s)∼iidN(0,IMs),zn(x)∼iidN(0,IMx),zn(y)∼iidN(0,IMy),xn∼N(Wxxzn(x)+Wxszn(s)+μx,  σ2IDx),yn∼N(Wyyzn(y)+Wyszn(s)+μy,  σ2IDy).\begin{align} \mbz_n^{(s)} &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{M_s}), & \mbz_n^{(x)} &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{M_x}), & \mbz_n^{(y)} &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{M_y}), \\ \mbx_n &\sim \cN(\mbW_{xx}\mbz_n^{(x)} + \mbW_{xs}\mbz_n^{(s)} + \mbmu_x,\; \sigma^2 \mbI_{D_x}), \\ \mby_n &\sim \cN(\mbW_{yy}\mbz_n^{(y)} + \mbW_{ys}\mbz_n^{(s)} + \mbmu_y,\; \sigma^2 \mbI_{D_y}). \end{align}zn(s)​xn​yn​​∼iidN(0,IMs​​),∼N(Wxx​zn(x)​+Wxs​zn(s)​+μx​,σ2IDx​​),∼N(Wyy​zn(y)​+Wys​zn(s)​+μy​,σ2IDy​​).​zn(x)​∼iidN(0,IMx​​),zn(y)​∼iidN(0,IMy​​),​
(17)

The shared latent variables zn(s)\mbz_n^{(s)}zn(s)​ capture common structure across modalities; private variables zn(x)\mbz_n^{(x)}zn(x)​ and zn(y)\mbz_n^{(y)}zn(y)​ capture modality-specific variation.

Bach & Jordan, 2005 showed that the MLE recovers the classical CCA solution: the shared weights Wxs\mbW_{xs}Wxs​ and Wys\mbW_{ys}Wys​ correspond to the left and right singular vectors of the sample cross-correlation matrix diag(Sxx)−1/2Sxydiag(Syy)−1/2\diag(\mbS_{xx})^{-1/2} \mbS_{xy} \diag(\mbS_{yy})^{-1/2}diag(Sxx​)−1/2Sxy​diag(Syy​)−1/2.

Conclusion¶

This chapter developed a family of linear Gaussian latent variable models with progressively richer structure:

ModelNoise covarianceLatent prior
PPCAσ2I\sigma^2 \mbIσ2I (isotropic)N(0,I)\cN(\mbzero, \mbI)N(0,I)
Factor analysisdiag(σ2)\diag(\mbsigma^2)diag(σ2) (diagonal)N(0,I)\cN(\mbzero, \mbI)N(0,I)
ICAdiag(σ2)\diag(\mbsigma^2)diag(σ2)∏mp(zm)\prod_m p(z_m)∏m​p(zm​) (non-Gaussian)
PCCAσ2I\sigma^2 \mbIσ2I (per modality)shared + private Gaussians

Key takeaways:

  • PCA is the MLE for PPCA: the principal components are the leading eigenvectors of the sample covariance, computed efficiently via SVD.

  • The posterior on latent variables requires only inverting an M×MM \times MM×M system (not D×DD \times DD×D), making inference tractable.

  • Gibbs sampling for PPCA / FA reduces to iterated Bayesian linear regressions — one per output dimension.

  • The non-Gaussian prior in ICA breaks the rotational indeterminacy of FA and enables recovery of independent source signals.

Next Steps

  • Variational Autoencoders — the nonlinear generalization: replace the linear decoder with a neural network

  • Linear Dynamical Systems — PPCA extended to sequential data; the Kalman filter is exact inference in a linear Gaussian SSM

Recommended Reading

Bishop, 2006, Ch 12.

References¶
  1. Bach, F. R., & Jordan, M. I. (2005). A probabilistic interpretation of canonical correlation analysis.
  2. Bishop, C. M. (2006). Pattern recognition and machine learning. Springer.
